schools.summary: The eighth day, the priesthood entering on its ministry: 'and it came to pass on the eighth day, that Moses called Aaron and his sons, and the elders of Israel' — on the eighth day (after the seven days of consecration, Leviticus 8:33-35), Moses calling Aaron, his sons, and the elders, that the priesthood might begin its public ministry (the eighth day, the day after the week of consecration, the first day of the priests' ministry, the calling, Moses summoning the priests and the elders to the inauguration of the priesthood, the figure, the eighth day, the new beginning, the day after the sabbath, the figure of a new order, perhaps of the resurrection, the eighth day of new creation); the eighth day (after the seven of consecration, the priesthood now entering its work, the new beginning, Leviticus 8:33) — the figure (the eighth day, the day of new beginning, the priesthood entering its ministry); 'Moses called Aaron and his sons, and the elders of Israel' (the priests and elders called, the inauguration of the priesthood, the people represented by the elders, the providence, the priesthood beginning its ministry, the people gathered) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And it came to pass on the eighth day, that Moses called Aaron and his sons, and the elders of Israel

schools.summary: The offerings commanded for Aaron himself: 'and he said unto Aaron, Take thee a young calf for a sin offering, and a ram for a burnt offering, without blemish, and offer them before the LORD' — Aaron to take a young calf for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering for himself, both without blemish (the calf for a sin offering, the atonement for Aaron's own sin, perhaps a reminder of the golden calf, Exodus 32, the ram for a burnt offering, the whole devotion, the without blemish, the unblemished victims, the figure of Christ, 1 Peter 1:19, the for himself, Aaron offering for his own sin first, vv.7); the calf for Aaron's sin offering (the atonement for himself, perhaps recalling the calf he made, Exodus 32) — the figure (the priest's own sin needing atonement, Hebrews 5:3); 'a ram for a burnt offering, without blemish, and offer them before the LORD' (the ram for the burnt offering, the whole devotion, after the atonement, the figure, the unblemished victims, Christ, 1 Peter 1:19, the providence, Aaron's offerings for himself, the atonement and devotion) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And he said unto Aaron, Take thee a young calf for a sin offering, and a ram for a burnt offering, without blemish, and offer them before the LORD

schools.summary: The offerings commanded for the people: 'and unto the children of Israel thou shalt speak, saying, Take ye a kid of the goats for a sin offering; and a calf and a lamb, both of the first year, without blemish, for a burnt offering' — the people to bring a kid of the goats for a sin offering, and a calf and a lamb for a burnt offering, all without blemish (the kid for a sin offering, the atonement for the people, the calf and lamb for a burnt offering, the whole devotion, the without blemish, the unblemished victims, the figure of Christ, the for the people, the offerings for the congregation, after Aaron's own, vv.7); the kid for the people's sin offering (the atonement for the congregation) — the figure (the atonement for the people); 'a calf and a lamb, both of the first year, without blemish, for a burnt offering' (the calf and lamb for the burnt offering, the whole devotion of the people, the figure, the unblemished victims, Christ, the providence, the people's offerings, the atonement and devotion) — JFB, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And unto the children of Israel thou shalt speak, saying, Take ye a kid of the goats for a sin offering; and a calf and a lamb, both of the first year, without blemish, for a burnt offering
the offerings commanded for the people
the people's atonement and whole devotion, the unblemished victims
the figure of Christ

schools.summary: The peace and meal offerings, and the promise of the LORD's appearing: 'also a bullock and a ram for peace offerings, to sacrifice before the LORD; and a meat offering mingled with oil: for to day the LORD will appear unto you' — the people also to bring a bullock and a ram for peace offerings, and a meal offering mingled with oil (the peace offerings, the fellowship and communion with God, the meal offering, the devotion, fine flour and oil, the full round of offerings, sin, burnt, peace, meal — the whole system, chs. 1-7); the appearing — 'for to day the LORD will appear unto you': the great promise, that on this day, when the offerings were rightly made, the LORD would appear (his glory, vv.23-24, the witnesses noting the significance — the LORD's appearing the goal of the worship, the offerings made that God might manifest his presence and acceptance, the glory appearing, vv.23-24, the lesson — the manifest presence of God the reward and end of right worship, God meeting his people in the appointed way, the glory revealed, the figure, the appearing of God's glory, the acceptance of the offerings, ultimately the appearing of God in Christ, John 1:14, and the hope of his appearing in glory, Titus 2:13; Hebrews 9:28); the LORD appearing the goal of the offerings (the glory to be manifested) — the providence (the worship that God might appear, the glory, vv.23-24) — Ellicott, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What is the significance of 'to day the LORD will appear unto you'?

sides.position: The LORD's manifest presence the goal and reward of right worship - the offerings rightly made that God might appear, the glory revealed (vv.23-24); a figure of the appearing of God in Christ (John 1:14) and the hope of his appearing in glory (Titus 2:13; Hebrews 9:28)

themes: Also a bullock and a ram for peace offerings, to sacrifice before the LORD; and a meat offering mingled with oil: for to day the LORD will appear unto you
the peace and meal offerings, and the promise of the LORD's appearing
the LORD's manifest presence the goal and reward of right worship (vv.23-24)
a figure of God appearing in Christ and the hope of his glory (John 1:14; Titus 2:13)

schools.summary: The congregation drawing near before the LORD: 'and they brought that which Moses commanded before the tabernacle of the congregation: and all the congregation drew near and stood before the LORD' — the people bringing the commanded offerings and drawing near, standing before the LORD (the bringing, the offerings brought as commanded, the drawing near, the congregation approaching, standing before God, the standing before the LORD, the people gathered for worship, awaiting the LORD's appearing, the figure, the congregation drawing near to God, the access through the offerings, Hebrews 10:22, 'let us draw near'); the offerings brought as commanded (the obedience) — the obedience (the people doing as commanded); 'all the congregation drew near and stood before the LORD' (the congregation approaching, standing before God, the worship, the access, the figure, the drawing near to God through the offerings, Hebrews 10:22, the providence, the people gathered, awaiting the LORD's appearing) — Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: The promise that the glory of the LORD shall appear: 'and Moses said, This is the thing which the LORD commanded that ye should do: and the glory of the LORD shall appear unto you' — the appearing — 'the glory of the LORD shall appear unto you': Moses declaring that, when they did as the LORD commanded, the glory of the LORD would appear (the witnesses noting the significance — the glory of the LORD the goal and reward of obedient worship, the manifest presence of God, the visible glory, to appear when the offerings were rightly made, vv.23-24, the lesson — the connection of obedience and the manifest presence, the glory appears to those who do as God commands, the worship in God's appointed way crowned with his manifest presence, 'this is the thing which the LORD commanded... and the glory shall appear' — obedience the way to the manifest glory, the figure — the glory of God revealed to the obedient, the manifest presence the end of worship, fulfilled in the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ, 2 Corinthians 4:6, and the hope of beholding his glory, John 17:24, the encouragement, the assurance that God would manifest his acceptance and presence); the doing as commanded (the obedience, the worship in God's way) — the obedience (the appointed worship, the condition of the glory appearing); the glory shall appear — the manifest presence (the providence, the glory the goal of the obedient worship, vv.23-24) — Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the promise of the glory appearing teach?

sides.position: The manifest presence of God the goal and reward of obedient worship - the glory to appear when the offerings were rightly made (vv.23-24), the connection of obedience and the manifest presence; a figure of the glory of God revealed in the face of Jesus Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6) and the hope of beholding his glory (John 17:24)

themes: And Moses said, This is the thing which the LORD commanded that ye should do: and the glory of the LORD shall appear unto you

schools.summary: Aaron offering for himself first, then the people: 'and Moses said unto Aaron, Go unto the altar, and offer thy sin offering, and thy burnt offering, and make an atonement for thyself, and for the people: and offer the offering of the people, and make an atonement for them; as the LORD commanded' — the priest's own offering — 'make an atonement for thyself, and for the people': Aaron to offer first for himself (his own sin offering and burnt offering), making atonement for himself, and then for the people (the witnesses noting the significance — the priest must make atonement for his own sin before he can offer for the people, Aaron himself a sinner, needing atonement first, the lesson — the Aaronic high priest's own sinfulness, the priest a sinner like the people, needing to offer first for himself, the imperfection of the Levitical priesthood, the contrast with Christ — the great difference from Christ the sinless High Priest, who needed no offering for himself, Hebrews 7:26-27, 'such an high priest became us, who is holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners... who needeth not daily, as those high priests, to offer up sacrifice, first for his own sins, and then for the people's: for this he did once, when he offered up himself'; Hebrews 5:3, the Aaronic priest 'ought... to offer for sins, as for the people, so also for himself,' the figure — the imperfect priest pointing to the perfect, the sinful mediator to the sinless); Aaron then offering for the people (the atonement for the congregation, after his own) — the order (himself first, then the people, the priest cleansed before interceding, the providence, the atonement for the people, the priest's mediation) — Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: Why must Aaron make atonement for himself first?

sides.position: The Aaronic high priest, himself a sinner, must offer first for his own sin before he can mediate for the people - the imperfection of the Levitical priesthood, in sharp contrast with Christ the sinless High Priest who needed no offering for himself but offered up himself once for all (Hebrews 7:26-27; 5:3)

themes: And Moses said unto Aaron, Go unto the altar, and offer thy sin offering, and thy burnt offering, and make an atonement for thyself, and for the people

in contrast with Christ the sinless High Priest who offered up himself (Hebrews 7:26-27; 5:3)

schools.summary: Aaron slaying his own sin offering: 'Aaron therefore went unto the altar, and slew the calf of the sin offering, which was for himself' — Aaron going to the altar and slaying the calf of his own sin offering (the going to the altar, Aaron beginning his priestly ministry, the slaying, the death of the victim, the atonement for himself, the for himself, the sin offering for Aaron's own sin, vv.7, the figure, the priest's own atonement, the imperfect priest, the calf perhaps recalling the golden calf, Exodus 32); Aaron going to the altar (beginning his ministry, the first sacrifice) — the ministry (Aaron entering on his priestly work); 'slew the calf of the sin offering, which was for himself' (the calf slain for Aaron's own sin, the atonement for himself, vv.7, the figure, the priest's own sin needing atonement, Hebrews 5:3, the providence, Aaron's atonement, the priest cleansed first) — Henry, Gill.

schools.summary: The blood of Aaron's sin offering applied to the altar: 'and the sons of Aaron brought the blood unto him: and he dipped his finger in the blood, and put it upon the horns of the altar, and poured out the blood at the bottom of the altar' — the blood of Aaron's sin offering put on the horns of the altar and poured at its base (the sons bringing the blood, the priests assisting, the blood on the horns, the atonement applied to the altar, the poured out at the base, the rest of the blood, the full giving of the life, the figure, the blood the means of atonement, the life poured out, Christ); the blood on the horns of the altar (the atonement applied, Leviticus 4:25) — the figure (the blood of atonement); 'poured out the blood at the bottom of the altar' (the rest of the blood poured, the full giving of the life, the figure, the life poured out, Isaiah 53:12, the providence, the blood applied and poured, the atonement) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

schools.summary: The fat of Aaron's sin offering burned: 'but the fat, and the kidneys, and the caul above the liver of the sin offering, he burnt upon the altar; as the LORD commanded Moses' — the fat, kidneys, and caul of Aaron's sin offering burned on the altar, as commanded (the fat burned, the best given to God, Leviticus 4:8-10, the inward parts, the kidneys and caul, the seat of the affections, the as commanded, the exact obedience, the figure, the best given to God, the inward devotion, even in the sin offering); the fat burned to God (the best, Leviticus 4:8) — the figure (the best, the inward, given to God); 'as the LORD commanded Moses' (the exact obedience, the fat burned as commanded, the figure, the obedience in the offering, the providence, the fat burned, the obedience kept) — Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: The body of Aaron's sin offering burned without the camp: 'and the flesh and the hide he burnt with fire without the camp' — the flesh and hide of Aaron's sin offering burned outside the camp (the body burned without the camp, the sin offering for the priest wholly burned outside, Leviticus 4:11-12, the not eaten, the priest's own sin offering not eaten by himself, the figure, the body burned without the camp, the figure of Christ suffering without the gate, Hebrews 13:11-13); the body burned (the flesh and hide, Leviticus 4:11) — the figure (the sin-bearing victim consumed); 'without the camp' (the body burned outside the camp, the place of rejection, Leviticus 4:12, the figure, Christ suffering without the gate, Hebrews 13:11-13, the providence, the body burned without the camp, the figure of Christ) — Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: The slaying of Aaron's burnt offering and the sprinkling of its blood: 'and he slew the burnt offering; and Aaron's sons presented unto him the blood, which he sprinkled round about upon the altar' — Aaron slaying the ram of his burnt offering, and his sons presenting the blood, which he sprinkled round about on the altar (the burnt offering, the whole offering of devotion, after the sin offering, the slaying, the death of the victim, the blood sprinkled round about, the atonement applied on every side, the figure, the burnt offering, the whole devotion, after the atonement, the blood, the atonement); the burnt offering slain (the whole devotion, Leviticus 1:5) — the figure (the whole offering, Christ wholly given); 'the blood, which he sprinkled round about upon the altar' (the blood sprinkled round about, the atonement applied, Leviticus 1:5, the figure, the blood of atonement, the providence, the burnt offering's blood, the devotion after the atonement) — Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: The burning of the pieces of Aaron's burnt offering: 'and they presented the burnt offering unto him, with the pieces thereof, and the head: and he burnt them upon the altar' — the burnt offering, cut in pieces with the head, presented and burned on the altar (the pieces and the head, the victim cut up, all burned, Leviticus 1:6-9, the burned, the whole offering consumed, the whole devotion, the figure, the whole offering wholly burned, Christ wholly given, Ephesians 5:2); the pieces and head presented (the victim cut up, Leviticus 1:8) — the whole offering (all burned); 'he burnt them upon the altar' (the whole burned, the whole offering consumed, the figure, the whole devotion, Christ wholly given, Ephesians 5:2, the providence, the burnt offering wholly burned) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Aaron offering the people's sin offering: 'and he brought the people's offering, and took the goat, which was the sin offering for the people, and slew it, and offered it for sin, as the first' — having offered for himself, Aaron then bringing the people's offering, slaying the goat of their sin offering, as he had done his own (the people's offering, after his own, the order, vv.7, the goat, the people's sin offering, the as the first, offered in the same manner as his own sin offering, the figure, the atonement for the people, after the priest's own, the order of mediation); the goat of the people's sin offering (the atonement for the congregation, after Aaron's own, vv.7) — the order (himself first, then the people); 'slew it, and offered it for sin, as the first' (the people's sin offering offered as Aaron's own, the same manner, the figure, the atonement for the people, the priest's mediation, the providence, the people's atonement, after the priest's own) — Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: The slaying of the people's peace offerings: 'he slew also the bullock and the ram for a sacrifice of peace offerings, which was for the people: and Aaron's sons presented unto him the blood, which he sprinkled upon the altar round about' — Aaron slaying the bullock and ram of the people's peace offerings, and sprinkling the blood on the altar (the peace offerings, the fellowship and communion with God, Leviticus 3, the slaying, the death of the victims, the blood sprinkled round about, the atonement applied, the figure, the peace offering, the fellowship with God, Romans 5:1, the communion of the people with God after the atonement); the peace offerings slain (the fellowship offering, Leviticus 3) — the figure (the peace and communion with God); 'the blood, which he sprinkled upon the altar round about' (the blood sprinkled round about, the atonement applied, Leviticus 3:2, the figure, the peace through the blood, Colossians 1:20, the providence, the peace offerings, the fellowship after the atonement) — Gill.

schools.summary: Aaron lifting up his hand and blessing the people: 'and Aaron lifted up his hand toward the people, and blessed them, and came down from offering of the sin offering, and the burnt offering, and peace offerings' — the priestly blessing — 'Aaron lifted up his hand toward the people, and blessed them': having finished the offerings, Aaron lifting his hands and blessing the people (the witnesses noting the significance — the priestly blessing, the high priest blessing the people, the part of his office, the lifted hands, the benediction, probably the Aaronic blessing, 'the LORD bless thee, and keep thee... the LORD make his face shine upon thee... the LORD lift up his countenance upon thee, and give thee peace,' Numbers 6:23-27, the lesson — the priest's office to bless, the high priest blessing the people after the atonement, the blessing flowing from the finished sacrifice, the figure — Christ the great High Priest blessing his people, who at his ascension 'lifted up his hands, and blessed them,' Luke 24:50-51, 'he led them out as far as to Bethany, and he lifted up his hands, and blessed them; and... while he blessed them, he was parted from them, and carried up into heaven,' the blessing of the finished work, the benediction of the High Priest after the atonement, the figure of Christ's continual blessing of his people from the finished sacrifice); Aaron coming down from the offerings (the sin, burnt, and peace offerings finished) — the order (the blessing after the finished offerings, the figure, the blessing flowing from the completed sacrifice) — Calvin, Wesley, Henry, Gill.

debates.question: What does Aaron's blessing of the people signify?

sides.position: The high priest's office to bless the people after the atonement (the Aaronic blessing, Numbers 6:23-27, the benediction flowing from the finished sacrifice) - a figure of Christ the great High Priest who at his ascension lifted up his hands and blessed his people (Luke 24:50-51), the blessing of the finished work

a figure of Christ who lifted up his hands and blessed his people at his ascension (Luke 24:50-51)

schools.summary: Moses and Aaron blessing the people and the glory of the LORD appearing: 'and Moses and Aaron went into the tabernacle of the congregation, and came out, and blessed the people: and the glory of the LORD appeared unto all the people' — Moses and Aaron going into the tabernacle (perhaps to present the offerings, or Moses to instruct Aaron in the inner service, or to pray), coming out, and blessing the people, and then the glory of the LORD appearing (the going in and out, the entering and returning, the blessing, the joint blessing, Moses and Aaron together, the glory appearing, the promised glory, vv.6, now revealed to all the people); Moses and Aaron blessing the people (the joint benediction, the law and the priesthood blessing) — the figure (the blessing of God's servants); the glory revealed — the glory of the LORD appeared to all the people, fulfilling the promise (vv.6, the witnesses noting the significance — the manifest presence of God, the glory revealed to all after the offerings and blessing, the visible glory, the shekinah, the token of God's acceptance and presence, the figure, the manifest glory after the finished worship, the presence of God revealed, the answer to the obedient worship, vv.6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Calvin, Henry, Gill.

themes: And Moses and Aaron went into the tabernacle of the congregation, and came out, and blessed the people: and the glory of the LORD appeared unto all the people
Moses and Aaron blessing the people and the glory of the LORD appearing
the manifest presence of God revealed to all after the offerings and blessing (vv.6)
the visible glory the token of God's acceptance, crowning the obedient worship

schools.summary: The fire from the LORD consuming the offering: 'and there came a fire out from before the LORD, and consumed upon the altar the burnt offering and the fat: which when all the people saw, they shouted, and fell on their faces' — the fire from heaven — 'there came a fire out from before the LORD, and consumed upon the altar the burnt offering and the fat': fire coming out from the LORD (from the cloud, the glory) and consuming the offering on the altar (the witnesses noting the significance — the divine acceptance of the sacrifice, the fire from heaven the visible token that God accepted the offering, consuming it, the sign of his acceptance and presence, the lesson — the fire from God the seal of acceptance, the sacrifice accepted, consumed by the divine fire, the answer of fire, as at other great moments, Genesis 15:17; Judges 6:21; 1 Kings 18:38, Elijah's offering; 1 Chronicles 21:26; 2 Chronicles 7:1, Solomon's temple, God answering by fire the sign of his acceptance, and this fire, once kindled from heaven, to be kept ever burning, Leviticus 6:13, never replaced by strange fire, Leviticus 10:1, the figure — the divine acceptance of the sacrifice, ultimately of Christ's offering accepted by God, the resurrection the Father's seal of acceptance, Romans 1:4; Acts 2:24, the fire of God's holiness consuming the offering, the acceptance of the atonement); the people's response — they shouted (with joy and praise) and fell on their faces (in awe and worship) — the joy and awe (the people rejoicing at the acceptance and falling in reverence at the manifest presence, the figure, the worship and joy at God's acceptance and glory) — Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does the fire from the LORD signify?

sides.position: The visible token of God's acceptance of the sacrifice (the answer of fire, as Genesis 15:17; 1 Kings 18:38; 2 Chronicles 7:1), the fire of God's holiness consuming the offering, kept ever burning thereafter (Leviticus 6:13) - a figure of the divine acceptance of the atonement and of Christ's offering accepted by God; the people's joy and awe the right response

themes: And there came a fire out from before the LORD, and consumed upon the altar the burnt offering and the fat: which when all the people saw, they shouted, and fell on their faces
the fire from the LORD consuming the offering (the crux of the divine acceptance)
the fire from heaven the token of God's acceptance, the answer of fire (Genesis 15:17; 1 Kings 18:38; 2 Chronicles 7:1)
a figure of the acceptance of the atonement, the people's joy and awe the right response
